pi_items.source: NAICS Code 5411 and 541110 confirm Legal Services is the correct industry classification for lawyers (https://www.ibisworld.com/classifications/naics/541110/)
O*NET OnLine confirms 23-1011.00 is the SOC code for Lawyers (https://www.onetonline.org/link/summary/23-1011.00)

pi_items.verified_value: Hadley v. Baxendale, 9 Ex. 341, 156 Eng. Rep. 145 (1854)
pi_items.source: Multiple legal case databases confirm this is a real 1854 English contract law case (https://www.quimbee.com/cases/hadley-v-baxendale)
Cornell Law School Legal Information Institute confirms U.C.C. § 2-715 (Buyer's Incidental and Consequential Damages) exists in 2002 Uniform Commercial Code (https://www.law.cornell.edu/ucc/2/2-715)

Multiple sources confirm Restatement (Second) of Contracts was published in 1981 and § 351 covers unforeseeability and limitations on damages (https://matthewminer.name/law/outlines/1L/2nd+Semester/LAW+506-002+%E2%80%93+Contracts+II/R2C+%C2%A7+351)
Multiple legal case databases confirm this is a real 1973 Massachusetts Supreme Judicial Court case (https://www.quimbee.com/cases/sullivan-v-o-connor)

privileged_info_summary: The task involves inserting four real legal citations as footnotes into a Word document. All citations are verified: (1) Hadley v. Baxendale, 9 Ex. 341, 156 Eng. Rep. 145 (1854) is a seminal English contract law case establishing the foreseeability rule for consequential damages. (2) U.C.C. § 2-715(2)(a) (2002) is the Uniform Commercial Code section on Buyer's Incidental and Consequential Damages. (3) Restatement (Second) of Contracts § 351 (1981) covers unforeseeability and limitations on damages in contract remedies. (4) Sullivan v. O'Connor, 363 Mass. 579, 296 N.E.2d 183 (1973) is a Massachusetts case involving breach of contract in the context of medical services. The metadata occupation (Lawyer), industry (Legal Services), and ONET-SOC code (23-1011.00) are all correctly aligned with U.S. Department of Labor classifications.
